coredata.dc:title: New bidental sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots modified with gold as a catalyst for hydrogen generation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Al-Azmi, Amal
Keshipour, Sajjad
coredata.dc:description: Disadvantages of fossil fuels encourage researchers to develop clean combustion sources, in particular, H2 due to the high potential energy and safe by-products. Herein, Au was deposited on S-doped graphene quantum dots to obtain a heterogeneous photocatalyst for the degradation of formic acid toward H2 generation. Insertion of thiol groups on graphene quantum dots was carried out by self-condensation reaction of citric acid in the presence of Dimaval, as the thiol groups carrier. After the complexation of Au with the prepared S-doped graphene quantum dots, the catalytic activity of composite was evaluated in formic acid degradation to generate H2 under visible light. Au@S-doped graphene quantum dots demonstrated superior catalytic activity with the turnover frequency up to 112 h−1. The reaction enjoys significant benefits such as stability and recyclability of the catalyst, excellent reaction rate, and mild reaction conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanostructure
Photocatalyst
Hydrogen generation
Graphene quantum dots
Green chemistry

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of Au on Au@S-doped GQD using FAAS
The typical procedure for the FA degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FTIR spectra of GQD and Au@S-doped GQD (A) and Raman spectrum of Au@S-doped GQDs.
Deconvoluted XPS spectra of Au@S-doped GQD for the peaks ascribed to Au, and S.
Elemental Mapping analysis of Au@S-doped GQD.
TEM images and elemental mapping (white Au and green carbon) of Au@S-doped GQD.
UV–Vis spectra of GQD. S-doped GQD, and Au@S-doped GQD (a), and PL of Au@S-doped GQD in various wavelengths and the sample fluorescence (b).

FA degradation toward H2/CO2 production: (a) Volume of the produced gases (H2 + CO2) as a function of reaction time, (b) volume of the produced gases (H2 + CO2) as a function of Au concentration at 10 min, (c) catalytic TOF of the reaction as a function of FA concentration at 10 min, (d) catalytic TOF of the reaction as a function of light power at 10 min, (e) catalytic TOF of the reaction as a function of temperature at 10 min, (f) volume of the produced gases (H2 + CO2) for various trials. In all graphs, the error bars show the standard deviation of three independent experiment.
The hydrogen generation mechanism from FA by Au@S-doped GQD.
CHNS elemental analysis of Au@S-doped GQD.
Comparison of the obtained results for FA degradation by Au@S-doped GQD with previous reports.

head.ce:title: New bidental sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots modified with gold as a catalyst for hydrogen generation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Kuwait University, P. O. Box 5969, Safat 13060, Kuwait
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry Department, Kuwait University, P. O. Box 5969, Safat 13060, Kuwait

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanotechnology, Faculty of Science, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Nanotechnology, Faculty of Science, Urmia University, Urmia, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Disadvantages of fossil fuels encourage researchers to develop clean combustion sources, in particular, H2 due to the high potential energy and safe by-products. Herein, Au was deposited on S-doped graphene quantum dots to obtain a heterogeneous photocatalyst for the degradation of formic acid toward H2 generation. Insertion of thiol groups on graphene quantum dots was carried out by self-condensation reaction of citric acid in the presence of Dimaval, as the thiol groups carrier. After the complexation of Au with the prepared S-doped graphene quantum dots, the catalytic activity of composite was evaluated in formic acid degradation to generate H2 under visible light. Au@S-doped graphene quantum dots demonstrated superior catalytic activity with the turnover frequency up to 112 h−1. The reaction enjoys significant benefits such as stability and recyclability of the catalyst, excellent reaction rate, and mild reaction conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Could H2 be considered as a trustworthy source of energy to obsolete using pollutant fossil fuels? Nobody could answer this question unless an efficient method is developed to produce H2, and this is a powerful motive for researchers to continue their studies on this energy source. Graphene derivatives are widely used in H2 production, accompanied by other catalysts, on account of their ability to enhance the electron injection efficiency and retard the charge recombination of catalysts.[1] These materials as the catalysts decrease the reaction time by promoting the reactions on numerous sites of their wide surfaces. Graphene quantum dot (GQD), the highest aspect ratio form of graphene derivatives, has been widely used in H2 generation in recent years.[2] Modified GQDs with transition metals and metal oxides are fascinating candidates for the H2 evolution. For instance, TiO2 indicated superior activity in combination with GQD to produce hydrogen.[3–6] Hydrolysis of NH3BH3 and NaBH4 by GQD decorated Rh nanoparticles (NPs), Ru NPs, and Pt NPs furnished H2 with turnover frequencies (TOF) of 656, 384, and 281 min−1, respectively.[7] The reaction for tetrahydroxyboran demonstrated the order of catalytic activity Pt NP > Rh NP > Au NP > Pd NP > Ir NP > Ru NP.[8] Simultaneous loading GQD and PdS enhanced ZnCdS catalytic activity with 517 mu mol.h−1 under visible light.[9] Nickel phosphide cocatalyst with GQD carried out the photocatalytic hydrogen generation from water under visible light, in which the existence of nickel phosphide enhanced the reaction rate about 94 times greater than that of bare GQD.[10] GQD/ZnNb2O6/g-C3N4 also promoted the photochemically water-splitting reaction with the rate of 340.9 mu mol.h−1.[11] Photoelectrochemical hydrogen production by CdS/GQD shed light on the fact that GQD improves the H2 production rate of CdS about four times.[12] The sole report about using Au/GQD in H2 evolution reaction is attributed to heating HAuCl4 with GQDs to obtain Au NPs supported on GQD, which was employed in electrochemical water-splitting.[13]

While a significant number of applications have been extended for GQDs, doping heteroatoms like sulfur (S) on these zero-dimension NPs more improved their uses.[14] Liu and co-workers performed the hydrothermal synthesis of S-doped GQDs using water-phase molecular fusion with 1,3,6-trinitropyrene, Na2S, and NaOH for highly selective and sensitive lead detection.[15] Sun et al. employed pyrolysis of citric acid (CA)/l-Cysteine to obtain N- and S-doped GQD to study electrochemiluminescence properties of the prepared compound.[16] To continue, this protocol was employed for the synthesis of a sensor for the detection of cyanide.[17] Sahiner et al. synthesized N- and S-doped carbon dots for biomedical applications under microwave conditions starting from CA and amino acids such as Arginine, Lysine, Histidine, Cysteine, and Methionine.[18] Moreover, the CA/Arginine pyrolysis product has been employed as an antibacterial agent after modification with Cu and Ag.[19] This product was also employed in the detection of thiamine.[20] In addition, the product of CA/Cysteamine hydrothermal reaction carried out the Co detection.[21] A research group conducted by Haridoss employed S- and N-doped GQDs with TiO2 for the oxygen reduction reaction.[22] Wu and co-workers employed pyrolyzing Aspartic acid/Cysteine to prepare a co-doped for bioimaging.[23] S-doped GQD, prepared from pyrolysis of CA/3-mercaptopropionic acid,[24] was also employed to detect 4-nitrophenol.[25] Chen et al. carried out CA pyrolysis with sodium phytate and sodium sulfate to produce P- and S-doped GQDs for monitoring nitrite in pickles.[26]

The use of two- or multi-dentate ligands on a support is a common strategy to obtain maximum efficiency of griping transition metals. To afford such supports, a significant number of amino acids were used to dope GQD, in which N and S atoms in these compounds with three bind distances (TBD) provide a suitable spatial arrangement to complex with a metal.[16–21] Our research group previously demonstrated that the existence of two thiol groups with TBD provides an exceptional opportunity for anchoring transition metals such as Ru.[27–29] Herein, we will point out the pyrolysis of CA/Dimaval to the synthesize of an S-doped TBD structure appropriate for anchoring gold to the final construction of Au supported on S-doped GQD (Au@S-doped GQD). The pyrolysis of CA is considered an efficient strategy for synthesizing of GQD compared to other approaches such as sulfur-assisted conversion of benzene into graphene[30] and low-temperature synthesis of edge-rich graphene on account of its easy workup and inexpensive starting material.[31] Therefore, the considered protocol could suggests some beneficial such as easy accessing of S-doped GQD, and creation of a TBD ligand by two S-atoms to efficiently supporting of Au. Since the Au(III) reduction to Au NPs needs reducing agents like NaBH4 and most of them pollute the product, this reduction was promoted by CA. So, as one of the notable achievements of this report compared to the previous reports is the dual applications of CA as the starting material of GQD and reducing agent of Au(III) to promote a one-pot synthesis, which in turn leads to the easy workup and reduces the loading of impurities on the product. With respect to the exceptional performance of GQD in the photocatalytic reactions,[9–13] Au@S-doped GQD could conduct on the photocatalytic degradation of formic acid (FA) for H2 generation.

As discussed before, S-Doped GQD has been synthesized via two distinct strategies, including the decoration of S during the host construction and deposition after synthesis of GQD. As a fact, the generation of GQD from CA provides the possibility of doping various atoms into its structure, avoiding exhausting multi-step reactions. In this research, thermally self-condensation reaction of CA in the presence of Dimaval afforded S-doped GQDs, in which the specific spatial arrangement of the thiol groups created a convenient place for perching of gold NPs to gain Au@S-doped GQD photocatalyst for the decomposition reaction of FA toward H2 generation (Scheme 1 ). It is worthy to note that, CA is in charge of Au(III) reduction to Au in addition to the leading role in the construction of GQD, giving to the one-pot synthesis of a complex composite, Au@S-doped GQD.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra were prepared for GQD and Au@S-doped GQD to investigate the success of the modification reaction (Fig. 1 A). Au@S-doped GQD indicated some peaks attributed to GQD, which could also be seen in the GQD spectrum. Peaks at about 3430, 2920/2850, 1760/1710, 1400, and 1190 cm−1 in both spectra are assigned to stretching OH, stretching CH, stretching CO, in-plane bending COH, and stretching CO, respectively.[32] It is worthy to note that, FT-IR spectrum of Au@S-doped GQD showed a characteristic stretching vibration mode at 2620 cm−1 for SH groups, which confirmed doping of thiol groups on GQD.[33] Furthermore, the Raman spectroscopy was employed to observe peaks related to D- and G-bands of GQD, in which both of these peaks appeared at 1359 and 1597 cm−1 in the spectrum, respectively (Fig. 1B). D- and G- bands are created due to defects in the hexagonal lattice and sp2 carbon atoms vibrations of GQD, respectively. The ratio of peaks intensities (ID/IG) contains remarkable information about the nature of graphene materials, in which the higher ratio indicates that the number of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups as the defects agents in the sheets is notable. Overall, graphene has a weaker D band or lower ID/IG compared to graphene oxide. For the Au@S-doped GQD, the ratio was calculated to be 1.29, demonstrating the presence of significant amounts of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the GQD. This fact is attributed to the use of CA as the starting material, which contains these functionalities. Moreover, a comparison between the obtained ratio for Au@S-doped GQD with GQD did not show any significant variations, indicating the stability of hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups during the modification reactions.[34]

1H Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy could provide valuable information about organic or organometallic structures like Au@S-doped GQD. The spectrum of Au@S-doped GQD demonstrated a characteristic peak at 2.93 ppm (Fig. 2 ), which did not appear in the GQD spectrum (supporting information). The modification appears to be well substantiated by this multiplet peak, which is split by protons 1 and 3. This result point to the fact that the modification was carried out successfully. The proposed structure could have been more confirmed if the peaks related to protons 1 and 3 did not overlap with GQD peaks.

To confirm the deposition of Au and Dimaval on the synthesized composite and the oxidation numbers of constructing atoms, X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out (Supporting information), which demonstrated deposition Au and Dimaval on GQD. The concentration on the spectrum around 87 eV indicated a doublet peak at 84.4 eV and 88.0 eV for Au 4F7/2 and Au 4F5/2, respectively. The binding energy for Au carries on significant information about the bonds when the thiol groups are incorporated in the material structure. The existence of S-atoms in the composite structure leads to the appearance of Au 4F7/2 peak at higher binding energy (about 84.5 eV) than a non-thiol structure with a peak at about 82 eV.[35] The peak at 84.4 eV for Au@S-doped GQD confirmed that Au atoms were bonded to the thiol groups. Similar trends have been reported for S 2p3/2, in which the S-atoms of Au-free materials and Au-composite give a peak at 161.5 eV and 162.4 eV, respectively. The existence of a peak at 162.4 eV in the XPS spectrum of Au@S-doped GQD approved the presence of S atoms bonded to Au. Furthermore, the peak of S 2p1/2 was appeared at 167.6 eV (Fig. 3 ).

XRD pattern of Au@S-doped GQD was provided to investigate the formation of GQD and Au NPs (Fig. 4 .). The pattern showed peaks at 2θ = 22.8⁰ related to the (002) of GQD, which is lower than the value reported for GQD prepared from CA at 24.2⁰. This shift suggests that the thiol groups have increased the interlayer spaces of GQDs in Au@S-doped GQD.[17] Moreover, the pattern displayed the peak of (111) for Au NPs.

The presence of C, O, S, and Au was also confirmed by Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX) analysis (Supporting information). Also, homogeneity of the prepared nanocomposite was confirmed by Elemental Mapping analysis, which revealed uniform distribution of all ingredients in the composite (Fig. 5 ). The homogeneous distribution of Au on the Au@S-doped GQD could provide more accessible reaction sites on the composite, leading to high catalytic activity.

CHNS elemental analysis was carried on Au@S-doped GQD (Table 1 ). The weight percentages of elements on Au@S-doped GQD were obtained C, 47.76%; H, 5.49%; and S, 3.52%. Regrading that molecular weight (M) of S is 32, 1 g of Au@S-doped GQD has 0.0011 mol (or 1.1 mmol) of S (eq. (1)). Considering that each Au needs two S to make a stable complex, the suitable sites for anchoring to Au is 0.55 mmol (eq. (2)). Moreover, Au loaded on the catalyst was also determined to be 0.51 mmol per 1 g of the catalyst according to Flame Atomic Absorption Spectroscopy (FAAS) analysis. The similarity of obtained results for CHNS elemental analysis and FAAS could result in all Au NPs attached to two thiols.
(1)
A =
S %
M
=
0.0352
32
= 0.0011 m o l
(2)
B =
molS
2
= 0.00055
where A is the mol of S per 1 g of composite and B is the mol of ideal sites for anchoring to Au.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) micrographs of Au@S-doped GQD revealed the synthesis of GQD NPs with the maximum size distribution between 16 and 22 nm (Fig. 6 ). More focusing on the observed NPs revealed the formation of Au NPs, which were recognizable from GQD NPs on account of mass-thickness contrast. Au NPs size distribution was distinguished in the range of 3–5 nm. It was necessary to reveal the nature of observed particles to approve the existence of GQD and Au NPs. Therefore, elemental mapping was carried out (Fig. 6), indicating presence of both the NPs via appearing carbons and Au atoms. The formation of NPs provides high surface area and finally leads to a high efficiency.

Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) on Au@S-doped GQD indicated two distinct weight losses with increasing temperature (Fig. 7 ). The first weight loss observed at 202 ⁰C seems attributed to the condensation of GQDs and releasing CO2/H2O. Another weight loss that led to the significant decrease in the catalyst mass was occurred at 603 ⁰C and is related to the deconstruction of the S-doped GQD structure.

UV–Vis spectra were provided for GQD, S-doped GQD, and Au@S-doped GQD to compare their optical absorption properties (Fig. 8 A). GQD demonstrated a broad absorption at 340 nm related to electron excitation from n → π* of CO bonds. This absorbance was shifted to 346 nm after doping S into GQD due to the interactions of S-atoms non-bonding electrons with the HOMO of GQD, leading to the HOMO energy level increasing and decreasing the bandgap. Finally, Au loading on S-doped GQD indicated a redshift to 324 nm by decreasing the HOMO energy level (or increasing bandgap) using unoccupied orbitals. In addition, a broad absorbance was observed in the range of under 300 nm related to π → π* for all of the studied compounds. The bandgap energies were calculated for GQD, S-doped GQD, and Au@S-doped GQD as 3.65, 3.59, and 3.83 eV, respectively.

The photoluminescence (PL) of Au@S-doped GQD was studied after irradiation with various wavelengths in the range of 310–410 nm (Fig. 8B). Strong emissions were happened around 513 nm by irradiation with wavelengths lying between 310 and 340 nm. Also, excitation with visible and UV lights was done on Au-S-doped GQD to investigate its optical properties, in which the solution color turned into green just under UV light (Fig. 8B).

The H2 production reaction under light is usually affected by a series of circumstances such as electron-donating system, irradiation wavelength, and photo-absorption system. [3–10] Au@S-doped GQD was considered for the H2 production since the composite would afford most of the desired conditions for the photocatalytic reaction. [6–10] High catalytic activity of Au, and electron injection by GQD, as well as its ability of retarding electron-hole recombination, could provide exceptional circumstances to conduct the degradation reaction. In this work, the photocatalytic activity of Au@S-doped GQD was investigated in H2 production from FA, as one of the valuable sources for hydrogen evolution.[36] The results exhibited high activity for Au@S-doped GQD in the deconstruction of FA, towards H2 generation under visible light providing 3.43 mmol.h−1 H2 using 30.6 µmol of the catalyst. H2 production was quenched in the absence of Au under visible irradiation, which approves the catalytic activity of this cation on the reaction. The reaction progress was monitored by the produced gasses amounts, which decreases in the gas production rate observed during the time, in particular, after 40 min (Fig. 9 a). Catalyst amounts screening showed a significant correlation between H2 generation rate and amounts of Au. Gas releasing from the mixture surprisingly increased with the increment in Au amounts, but for catalyst amounts higher than 30.6 mmol, indicating that 30 mmol of Au is the best reaction conditions (Fig. 9b). Moreover, the reaction yield was impressed by the concentration of FA with 0.7 M affording TOF of up to 112 h−1 (Fig. 9c). As an eminent factor on photocatalytic reaction, the power of the employed light source was evaluated on TOF of the reaction, in which the beneficial effect of higher powers was found. In this regard, a light source with powers of 100, 200, and 300 W afforded TOF of up to 67, 112, and 166 h−1, respectively (Fig. 9d). The temperature effect was evaluated on the reaction, assuming that the reaction is not diffusion-limited below 60 ⁰C. Temperature had an significant influence on the catalytic activity of Au@S-doped GQD, where a high temperature was beneficial for the dehydrogenation reaction (Fig. 9e). Moreover, the activation energy (Ea) was calculated to be 3.2 Kj.mol−1 for the reaction by Au@S-doped GQD.

Potential Au leaching into the mixture of FA degradation reaction was also analyzed with FAAS analysis. For this purpose, filtrate of the heterogeneous degradation reaction of FA after 1 h was passed from a syringe filter and then dissolved in HNO3. FAAS analysis of the sample evinced that the Au concentration in the reaction mixture was less than the detection limit. This result indicates that virtually no Au leaches from Au@S-doped GQD into the mixture. A hot filtration test was also performed on Au@S-doped GQD by removing the catalyst from the reaction mixture after 10 min and monitoring the reaction progress. Under this condition, gas releasing was quenched entirely, confirming heterogeneously doing of the reaction. Next, the catalyst stability was examined in the reaction mixture. The light source was removed after 1 h, and irradiation was performed again after 24 h with 3.43 mmol.h−1 H2, which showed that Au@S-doped GQD is a stable catalyst in FA. Also, the catalyst recyclability was investigated for 6 cycles with 5 successive trials by the recovered catalyst (Fig. 9f). The gas releasing indicated a meaningful decrease in the 6th cycle, confirming that the catalyst was recyclable for 5 cycles. The stability of Au@S-doped GQD in the reaction mixture was confirmed with FT-IR spectroscopy, in which the spectrum of recovered catalyst did not show any significant changes compared to the fresh catalyst (ESI).

The proposed mechanism of the hydrogen generation from CA by Au@S-doped GQD was represented in Scheme 2 . The high performance of Au in combination of S-doped GQD suggests that these materials have a synergistic effect to separation of electrons/holes, which is in relation with the formation of interface between them. The excitation of semiconductor, S-doped GQD, by photo irradiation creates electron/holes on the conduction band and the valence band, respectively, which in turn leads to the Mott–Schottky junction in the middle of S-doped GQD and Au NPs interfaces.[37] This embarks on the establishment of new Fermi level with the energy level lower than the conduction band of S-doped GQD.[38] The Fermi level is charged with electrons provided by the conduction band of S-doped GQD and the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) state of Au, which leads to more separation of electrons and holes. The electrons in the Fermi level are in charge of hydrogen evolution from H+ and the holes in the valance band of the semiconductor run the CO2 production by an oxidation way.[39]

Finally, the results of FA degradation were compared with some of the previous reports, which represent innovative alternatives to hydrogen production (Table 2 ). Au supported on silicazirconia is a significant example of the demonstrating the catalytic activity of Au in the deconstruction reaction of FA.[40] This catalyst provided high TOF than Au@S-doped GQD, but they need high temperatures promote the reaction. Ru-loaded CdS/Al-HMS promoted the reaction with low TOF, although it enjoys mild conditions and photo-induced promotion.[41] Pd catalysts of Pd/C,[42] Pd-MnOx/SiO2-NH2,[43] and Pd@carbon nitride[44] catalyzed the reaction with the TOFs up to 48, 140, 191, respectively. Decomposition of FA with Pd catalysts has the advantages of mild reaction conditions similar to this report. Some of the drawbacks of these reactions compared to our work are low TOF of Pd/C, tedious and difficult preparation of Pd-MnOx/SiO2-NH2, and depending on the reaction efficiency for the use of sodium formate as an additive in Pd@carbon nitride catalyzed reaction.

S-doped GQD was synthesized through pyrolysis of CA in the presence of Dimaval and then modified with gold NPs through reduction of Au(III) by CA. We believed that the use of CA as the starting material of GQD and also reducing agent of Au(III) could provide an opportunity to one-pot construction of a relatively complex catalyst, Au@S-doped GQD. Surprisingly, Au@S-doped GQD was produced via a one-pot protocol, in which the remaining CA from condensation reaction for the GQD synthesis was conducted on the reduction of Au(III). In the meantime, doping of S atoms with a three-bind distance provided an appropriate spatial arrangement for supporting Au. The synthesized composite was used as a photocatalyst for the degradation of FA towards H2 generation. In this reaction, S-doped GQD as a semiconductor provided electrons/holes to assist Au in the transforming of FA to H2 and CO2. The existence of GQD as a support provided convenient conditions for performing a photo-induced reaction on account of its capability to inject electrons/holes to the catalytic system and retard the recombination of electron-hole. The photocatalyst carried out the reaction at room temperature with the TOF of up to 112 h−1. The strategy used for the synthesis of Au@S-doped GQD could be employed to construct of a wide variety of catalysts, especially for the metals like Pd, which are reduced by CA. Moreover, the structure of GQD could be modified by different heteroatoms via our approach to obtain lower bandgaps.

All reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification. Tetrachloroauric(III) with the purity of ≥ 99.9%, citric acid with the purity of ≥ 99.5%, 2,3-dimercapto-1-propanesulfonic acid sodium salt monohydrate 95% (Dimaval) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich company. TEM micrographs were obtained with Philips CM100 BioTWIN transmission electron microscope and TEM Philips EM 208S. Au determination was carried out on a FAAS (Shimadzu 105 model AA-680 atomic absorption spectrometer) with a hollow cathode lamp. The elemental analysis was performed using Elemental Analyzer- Elementar. The GC high-resolution gas chromatograph mass spectrometry was carried out using a Thermo Scientific model. FT-IR was used to characterize the different functional groups of the composite using an AVATAR FTIR instrument of Thermo company in the range of 600–4000 cm−1. NMR of Varian - INOVA 500 MHz gave the 1H NMR spectra and both spectra were collected in d6 -DMSO as the solvent. FlexPS equip (Specs) was used during the XPS measurements employing a monochromatic Al Kα (hν = 1486.6 eV) source, 100 W power and 10 kV voltage. The scans were acquired with a pass-energy of 20 eV, 20 scans per sample. The instrument work function was calibrated using C1s photoelectron. Spectra were analyzed using Casa XPS software. UV–Vis spectra were obtained by WPA Biowave LifeScience UV–Vis spectrometer using quartz cuvettes with a path-length of 10 mm in H2O as the solvent.

CA (2.00 g) and Dimaval (0.5 g) were added to a vessel, and heated at 200 °C for 30 min. The heating was then stopped, and after cooling the mixture to room temperature, HAuCl4·3H2O (0.25 g) was added to the vessel, and the mixture was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Next, NaOH (0.1 N, 10 ml) was added to the vessel and instantly, 20 ml of acetone was added to the vessel to precipitate Au@S-doped GQD.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Au on Au@S-doped GQD using FAAS
ce:section.ce:para: Au@S-doped GQD (0.10 g) was added to a mixture of HCl:HNO3 (3:1) (10.0 ml) and sonicated for 3 h. Next, the total volume of the filtrate was increased to 20 ml with distilled water. The final solution was aspirated into the AAS flame against the blank prepared with S-doped GQD. The Au concentration was obtained using a calibration curve prepared with Au solution standards.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The typical procedure for the FA degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
Photocatalytic degradation of FA was done by a 200 W xenon lamp (λ > 420 nm). Initially, 0.06 g (30.6 µmol) of Au@S-doped GQD was added to 30 ml of FA. Then to disperse catalysts, the mixture was stirred without any light irradiation for 30 min. Next, the mixture is exposed to light irradiation to perform a photochemical reaction while continuously being stirred. The reaction vessel was linked by a hose to an inverted burette filled with water to trap the produced gas, and the volume of H2 produced was calculated assuming both H2 and CO2 act as ideal gases. TOF was calculated in 10 min by the following equation:
TOF
(
h
- 1
)
=
( P V )
/
( 2 R . T .
n
Au
. t )
where P is atmospheric pressure (atm), V is the volume of produced gas (ml), R is the universal gas constant (0.08206 L.atm/mol.K), T is temperature (K), nAu is the mmol of Au in the used catalyst, and t is time (h)

Al-Azmi designed the study, prepared the material, conducted the experiments, performed analyses, and revised the paper; S. Keshipour designed the study, analysed the result, wrote the paper, and revised the paper.

Amal Al-Azmi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Sajjad Keshipour: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the facilities provided by Kuwait University through RSPU projects GS 01/05 and GS 03/01.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.01.005.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene-based materials for hydrogen generation from light-driven water splitting
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Xie, K. Zhang, B. Guo, Q. Liu, L. Fang, J.R. Gong, Graphene-Based Materials for Hydrogen Generation from Light-Driven Water Splitting, Advanced Materials 25(28) (2013) 3820-3839.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Graphene and graphene-like materials for hydrogen energy
Nanotechnol. Russ.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: O.K. Alekseeva, I.V. Pushkareva, A.S. Pushkarev, V.N. Fateev, Graphene and Graphene-Like Materials for Hydrogen Energy, Nanotechnologies in Russia 15(3) (2020) 273-300.
